skill.name: Azure.Messaging.ServiceBus (.NET)
skill.description: Azure Service Bus SDK for .NET. Enterprise messaging with queues, topics, subscriptions, and sessions.
skill.summary: Build reliable message-driven .NET applications with Azure Service Bus queues, topics, and sessions.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This is a prompt-only documentation skill with no executable code. Static analysis correctly identified zero security risks. The skill provides legitimate Azure Service Bus SDK guidance including authentication best practices (DefaultAzureCredential), proper resource disposal patterns, and standard messaging workflows. No suspicious patterns, injection attempts, or malicious intent detected.

content.user_title: Build Azure Service Bus messaging solutions in .NET
content.value_statement: Developers struggle with reliable message delivery and pub/sub patterns in distributed .NET applications. This skill provides complete Azure Service Bus SDK guidance with production-ready code examples.
actual_capabilities: Send and receive messages from Azure Service Bus queues and topics
Implement pub/sub patterns with topics and subscriptions
Configure background message processing with ServiceBusProcessor
Handle sessions for ordered FIFO message processing
Manage dead letter queues and message settlement
Create and configure queues, topics, and subscriptions via administration client
limitations: Requires Azure subscription and Service Bus namespace
Authentication requires Azure credentials or connection strings
Does not execute code - provides guidance and code examples only
Network access required to connect to Azure Service Bus endpoints

use_cases.title: Enterprise Message Queue Integration
use_cases.description: Build reliable asynchronous communication between microservices using Service Bus queues with proper error handling and message settlement.

use_cases.title: Event-Driven Architecture with Topics
use_cases.description: Implement publish/subscribe patterns for broadcasting events to multiple subscribers across different services.

use_cases.title: Ordered Processing with Sessions
use_cases.description: Process related messages in strict order using session-based receivers for scenarios like order fulfillment workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Queue Messaging
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to send and receive a single message from an Azure Service Bus queue using .NET with DefaultAzureCredential authentication.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner learning basic Service Bus operations

prompt_templates.title: Background Message Processor
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a ServiceBusProcessor that handles messages from a queue with concurrent calls, proper error handling, and automatic message completion.

prompt_templates.title: Session-Based Ordered Processing
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement session-based message processing in .NET that maintains FIFO order for related messages using ServiceBusSessionReceiver.

prompt_templates.title: Topic Subscription with Filters
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up Azure Service Bus topics with multiple subscriptions and SQL filters to route messages to different subscribers based on message properties.

output_examples.input: Send a message to Azure Service Bus queue
output: Code example showing ServiceBusClient creation with DefaultAzureCredential
ServiceBusSender initialization for the target queue
Async SendMessageAsync call with proper disposal pattern
ServiceBusProcessor configuration with AutoCompleteMessages and MaxConcurrentCalls
ProcessMessageAsync handler with try/catch and message settlement
ProcessErrorAsync handler for error logging and diagnostics

best_practices: Use DefaultAzureCredential for authentication instead of connection strings in production environments
Reuse ServiceBusClient, senders, and receivers as singletons - they are thread-safe and designed for long-lived usage
Always dispose resources using await using statements to ensure proper connection cleanup
anti_patterns: Creating new ServiceBusClient instances for each message - causes connection exhaustion and performance degradation
Using connection strings with shared access keys in client applications - exposes credentials and lacks fine-grained access control
Ignoring message lock duration - messages may be abandoned and redelivered if processing exceeds the lock timeout

faq.question: What is the difference between queues and topics in Azure Service Bus?
faq.answer: Queues provide one-to-one messaging where each message is consumed by a single receiver. Topics enable one-to-many publish/subscribe patterns where multiple subscriptions can receive copies of the same message.

faq.question: How do I handle message processing failures?
faq.answer: Use the processor pattern with try/catch blocks. On failure, call AbandonMessageAsync to release the lock for retry, or DeadLetterMessageAsync to move poisoned messages to the dead letter subqueue after max delivery count is exceeded.

faq.question: When should I use sessions?
faq.answer: Use sessions when you need FIFO ordering for related messages, such as processing all events for a specific order or customer in sequence. Set SessionId on messages and use AcceptSessionAsync or AcceptNextSessionAsync.

faq.question: What authentication method should I use?
faq.answer: Use DefaultAzureCredential with Microsoft Entra ID for production. It supports managed identities, Azure CLI credentials, and Visual Studio credentials. Connection strings should only be used for local development.

faq.question: How do I monitor Service Bus message processing?
faq.answer: Enable diagnostic logging in the Azure portal, use Application Insights for telemetry, and implement the ProcessErrorAsync handler to capture processing errors and entity information.

faq.question: What happens to messages that fail processing?
faq.answer: Messages are automatically requeued if the lock expires or AbandonMessageAsync is called. After MaxDeliveryCount attempts, messages move to the dead letter queue. Use DeadLetteringOnMessageExpiration to auto-dead-letter expired messages.
